State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5660 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

September 30, 1982


Grant H. Kenyon
Legislative Advocate
1228 N Street, Suite 5
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Advice on Reporting by
Retained Entity in Response to
Your Letter of September 13,
1982, Our No. A-82-154

Dear Mr. Kenyon:

Thank you for your letter requesting the Commission's advice
regarding your reporting requirements under the lobbyist
reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act.1/ The
facts, as stated in your letter are as follows:

FACTS

You are and have been a registered lobbyist for many years.
While you are a sole practitioner, you have incorporated your
business, so that the corporation actually employs you and all
receipts from your clients are made payable to Grant H. Kenyon,
Inc. You have stated that you are "the sole shareholder and
employee" of Grant H. Kenyon, Inc. However, elsewhere in your
letter you make reference to your secretary.

In a recent Franchise Tax Board (FTB) audit, they advised
you that under these circumstances, you should be filing
lobbyist reports as a "retained entity" under our regulation
9 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18619. Instead, you have been filing
only lobbyist reports (our Form 620). You have based your past
reporting practices "on legal advice obtained, which was
believed to be in agreement with the FPPC Advice Letter by

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
specified.

Grant H. Kenyon
Septemoer 30, 1982
Page 2


Executive Director Michael Bennett to Sherman C. Wilke issued
May 17, 1976."2/

QUESTION

You have asked whether you must also file reports as a
retained entity and have expressed concerns over whether your
clients and secretary may become "confused" if you are required
to file an "additional report."

ANALYSIS

Grant H. Kenyon, Inc., is the employer of a lobbyist (you)
and meets all of the qualifications for a retained entity. The
specific factors involved in the Wilke letter are not present in
your situation. Your clients retain your firm3/ and pay your
firm. Such was not the case in the Wilke letter. You are an
employee of your firm and apparently employ a secretary as well.

A review of your recent filings (Form 620) shows a very
large lump sum "general lobbying expense" category. On your
Form 620 report this is not broken down to show payments to your
secretary, for office rental, phones, general meals and support,
etc. This information would be properly disclosed if you were
filing also as a retained entity. Consequently, it is our
conclusion that you must file reports as a retained entity as
well as the lobbyist reports.

However, you need not file both Form 620 and 650. The
Commission has a form (Form 660 - Consolidated Report of
Lobbyist and Lobbyist Employer) which you may file.4/ In that
way, with one combined report you will be able to properly and
fully disclose both your receipts and disbursements. In
addition, you should be conscious of the provisions of our

_______________________________

2/ This is our advice letter No. A-76-531, copy enclosed
together with a copy of Mr. Wilke's letter, containing the
factual circumstances involved.

3/ Grant H. Kenyon, Inc.

4/ Copies enclosed. Please note you must also attach our
Form 680.

Grant H. Kenyon
September 30, 1982
Page 3


regulations5/ relating to the appropriate sums to be reported
as your compensation6/ and that of your secretary.

CONCLUSION

You are required by the provisions of the Political Reform
Act and the Commission's regulations to file lobbying reports as
both a lobbyist and as a retained entity. You may do this
either by filing both a Form 620 and a Form 650 or, in the
alternative, by filing a Form 660 with a Form 680 attached.

Should you have any questions regarding this letter, please
do not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Robert B. Leidigh
Counsel
Legal Division

REL:plh
Enclosures


5 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18620(b) and (d), copy
enclosed.

6/ For instance, this would not include certain fringe
benefits which your firm may provide for you.

GRANT H. KENYON
LEGISLATIVE ADVOCATE

1228 N STREET, SUITE 5
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814

(916) 442-5773

September 13, 1982


Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California  95804

Re:  Advice on Structuring a Sole Proprietorship
     Corporation to Avoid Being a Retained Entity

Gentlemen:

     Pursuant to Government Code Sec. 83114, I request an
opinion from the Commission with regard to the following factual
situation.  I have been a registered lobbyist for many years and,
since the inception of the Political Reform Act, have filed
lobbyist reports Form 620 on a timely basis.  I have always
identified all clients, the fees and expenses received from them
and my general and itemized disbursements as required by your
regulations.

     It is my understanding that on or about August 1, 1975,
the FPPC adopted Regulation 18619, in an attempt to better dis-
close situations where a client did not hire a lobbyist but hired
an entity which employed one or more lobbyists.  The regulation
named such lobbying, law, public relations firm, etc., as a
"retained entity" and required that it also file as an Employer
of Lobbyist with a schedule of client information attached, such
that the entire chain of accounting is reported.

     Since I have always been a sole practitioner and all
income received by me is for my expenses and profit, the "retained
entity" concept seems inapplicable.  However, for my own tax
planning, I did incorporate as a professional corporation and have
directed my clients to make checks payable to Grant H. Kenyon,
Inc., of which I am an officer and the sole shareholder and

Fair Political Practices Commission - page 2 - September 13, 1982


employee. Based on legal advice obtained, which was believed to
be in agreement with the FPPC Advice Letter by Executive Director
Michael Bennett to Sherman C. Wilke issued on May 17, 1976, I
continued to file reports in the manner that I have always done.

In a recent audit with the Franchise Tax Board, I was
told that despite a disagreement between various auditors in that
office, I would be considered out of compliance with the Political
Reform Act if I did not discontinue my practice of reporting all
income as received by me and all expenses as paid by me and change
to the retained entity format requiring me to file an additional
Form 650 to report for my corporation separate from the Form 620
report filed by me. The Franchise Tax Board's audit of my 1981
records showed all transactions properly reported on Form 620's,
but will reportedly include a "nonmaterial finding" for not filing
Form 650's. They said the next audit will indicate noncompliance
if no Form 650's are filed unless the Commission or its staff
confirms that I am not required to file as a retained entity.

I believe I presently have a system that allows timely
and accurate filing. The requirement to add an additional report
to this system which may confuse my clients and my secretary, may
cause problems and therefore perhaps inaccurate filings. The need
to change appears nonsensical to me because no relevant new information will be obtained by such change, except that my report will
appear that I am part of a lobbying firm instead of a sole
practitioner, which impression will be inaccurate.

I hope you agree that my present filing format complies
with the Act and I request confirmation of this fact such that I
can send your letter to the FTB and not have to make changes in my
bookkeeping and filing procedures.

I appreciate your timely response to this request.

Sincerely,

Grant H. Kenyon

September 14, 1982


Grant H. Kenyon
Legislative Advocate
1228 N St., Ste. 5
Sacramento, CA 95814

                                                    Re: A-82-154

Dear Mr. Kenyon:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Robert Leigdh, an attorney
in the Legal Division of the Fair Political Practices
Commission. If you have any questions about your advice
request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

Sacramento, California 95804

Re: Advice on Structuring a Sole Proprietorship
Corporation to Avoid Being a Retained Entity

Gentlemen:

Pursuant to Government Code Sec. 83114, I request an
opinion from the Commission with regard to the following factual
situation. I have been a registered lobbyist for many years and,
since the inception of the Political Reform Act, have filed
lobbyist reports Form 620 on a timely basis. I have always
identified all clients, the fees and expenses received from them
and my general and itemized disbursements as required by your
regulations.

It is my understanding that on or about August 1, 1975,
the FPPC adopted Regulation 18619, in an attempt to better dis-
close situations where a client did not hire a lobbyist but hired
an entity which employed one or more lobbyists. The regulation
named such lobbying, law, public relations firm, etc., as a
"retained entity" and required that it also file as an Employer
of Lobbyist with a schedule of client information attached, such
that the entire chain of accounting is reported.

Since I have always been a sole practitioner and all S.P.
income received by me is for my expenses and profits, the "retained
entity" concept seems inapplicable. However, for my own tax
planning, I did incorporate as a professional corporation and have
directed my clients to make checks payable to Grant H. Kenyon,
Inc., of which I am an officer and the sole shareholder and

Fair Political Practices Commission - page 2 - September 13, 1982


employee. Based on legal advice obtained, which was believed to
be in agreement with the FPPC Advice Letter by Executive Director
Michael Bennett to Sherman C. Wilke issued on May 17, 1976, I
continued to file reports in the manner that I have always done.

     In a recent audit with the Franchise Tax Board, I was
told that despite a disagreement between various auditors in that
office, I would be considered out of compliance with the Political
Reform Act if I did not discontinue my practice of reporting all
income as received by me and all expenses as paid by me and change
to the retained entity format requiring me to file an additional
Form 650 to report for my corporation separate from the Form 620
report filed by me.  The Franchise Tax Board's audit of my 1981
records showed all transactions properly reported on Form 620's,
but will reportedly include a "nonmaterial finding" for not filing
Form 650's.  They said the next audit will indicate noncompliance
if no Form 650's are filed unless the Commission or its staff
confirms that I am not required to file as a retained entity.

     I believe I presently have a system that allows timely
and accurate filing.  The requirement to add an additional report
to this system which may confuse my clients and my secretary, may
cause problems and therefore perhaps inaccurate filings.  The need
to change appears nonsensical to me because no relevant new infor-
mation will be obtained by such change, except that my report will
appear that I am part of a lobbying firm instead of a sole
practitioner, which impression will be inaccurate.

     I hope you agree that my present filing format complies
with the Act and I request confirmation of this fact such that I
can send your letter to the FTB and not have to make changes in my
bookkeeping and filing procedures.

     I appreciate your timely response to this request.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Grant H. Kenyon

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest

November 2, 1982


Grant Kenyon
Grant H. Kenyon, Inc.
Legislative Advocate
1228 N Street, Suite 5
Sacramento, CA 95814

                                        Re:  Prior Advice Letter,
                                             Our No. A-82-154

Dear Mr. Kenyon:

     You have telephoned requesting clarification of an
aspect of my September 30, 1982, advice letter to you. You
have asked whether you may change your lobbying reporting
in accordance with the advice in my letter beginning with
reports for calendar year 1983. Your concern is that three
quarterly reports for 1982 have already been filed, with
one remaining. Altering your filing methodology at this
point would involve substantial additional work on your
part, particularly as to cumulative totals.

     After discussing the matter with Jim Stevens, Franchise
Tax Board, Political Reform Audit Division, I have advised
you that you may file your last quarter 1982 (due by
January 31, 1983) in the same manner that you have in the
past. Your 1983 reports will be done in accordance with
the advice in my letter of September 30.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Robert E. Ledgich
                                        Robert E. Ledgich
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

sections.question: You have asked whether you must also file reports as a retained entity and have expressed concerns over whether your clients and secretary may become "confused" if you are required to file an "additional report."
sections.conclusion: You are required by the provisions of the Political Reform Act and the Commission's regulations to file lobbying reports as both a lobbyist and as a retained entity. You may do this either by filing both a Form 620 and a Form 650 or, in the alternative, by filing a Form 660 with a Form 680 attached.
sections.facts:
You are and have been a registered lobbyist for many years. While you are a sole practitioner, you have incorporated your business, so that the corporation actually employs you and all receipts from your clients are made payable to Grant H. Kenyon, Inc. You have stated that you are "the sole shareholder and employee" of Grant H. Kenyon, Inc. However, elsewhere in your letter you make reference to your secretary.

In a recent Franchise Tax Board (FTB) audit, they advised you that under these circumstances, you should be filing lobbyist reports as a "retained entity" under our regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18619. Instead, you have been filing only lobbyist reports (our Form 620). You have based your past reporting practices "on legal advice obtained, which was believed to be in agreement with the FPPC Advice Letter by

. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise specified.

Executive Director Michael Bennett to Sherman C. Wilke issued May 17, 1976.
sections.analysis:
Grant H. Kenyon, Inc., is the employer of a lobbyist (you) and meets all of the qualifications for a retained entity. The specific factors involved in the Wilke letter are not present in your situation. Your clients retain your firm3/ and pay your firm. Such was not the case in the Wilke letter. You are an employee of your firm and apparently employ a secretary as well.

A review of your recent filings (Form 620) shows a very large lump sum "general lobbying expense" category. On your Form 620 report this is not broken down to show payments to your secretary, for office rental, phones, general meals and support, etc. This information would be properly disclosed if you were filing also as a retained entity. Consequently, it is our conclusion that you must file reports as a retained entity as well as the lobbyist reports.

However, you need not file both Form 620 and 650. The Commission has a form (Form 660 - Consolidated Report of Lobbyist and Lobbyist Employer) which you may file.4/ In that way, with one combined report you will be able to properly and fully disclose both your receipts and disbursements. In addition, you should be conscious of the provisions of our

2/ This is our advice letter No. A-76-531, copy enclosed together with a copy of Mr. Wilke's letter, containing the factual circumstances involved.

3/ Grant H. Kenyon, Inc.

4/ Copies enclosed. Please note you must also attach our Form 680.

regulation5/ relating to the appropriate sums to be reported as your compensation6/ and that of your secretary.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have asked whether you must also file reports as a retained entity and have expressed concerns over whether your clients and secretary may become "confused" if you are required to file an "additional report."

CONCLUSION: You are required by the provisions of the Political Reform Act and the Commission's regulations to file lobbying reports as both a lobbyist and as a retained entity. You may do this either by filing both a Form 620 and a Form 650 or, in the alternative, by filing a Form 660 with a Form 680 attached.

BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest (916) 322-5660 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 September 30, 1982 Grant H. Kenyon Legislative Advocate 1228 N Street, Suite 5 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Advice on Reporting by Retained Entity in Response to Your Letter of September 13, 1982, Our No. A-82-154 Dear Mr. Kenyon: Thank you for your letter requesting the Commission's advice regarding your reporting requirements under the lobbyist reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act.1/ The facts, as stated in your letter are as follows: FACTS You are and have been a registered lobbyist for many years. While you are a sole practitioner, you have incorporated your business, so that the corporation actually employs you and all receipts from your clients are made payable to Grant H. Kenyon, Inc. You have stated that you are "the sole shareholder and employee" of Grant H. Kenyon, Inc. However, elsewhere in your letter you make reference to your secretary. In a recent Franchise Tax Board (FTB) audit, they advised you that under these circumstances, you should be filing lobbyist reports as a "retained entity" under our regulation 9 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18619. Instead, you have been filing only lobbyist reports (our Form 620). You have based your past reporting practices "on legal advice obtained, which was believed to be in agreement with the FPPC Advice Letter by 1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise specified. Grant H. Kenyon Septemoer 30, 1982 Page 2 Executive Director Michael Bennett to Sherman C. Wilke issued May 17, 1976."2/ QUESTION You have asked whether you must also file reports as a retained entity and have expressed concerns over whether your clients and secretary may become "confused" if you are required to file an "additional report." ANALYSIS Grant H. Kenyon, Inc., is the employer of a lobbyist (you) and meets all of the qualifications for a retained entity. The specific factors involved in the Wilke letter are not present in your situation. Your clients retain your firm3/ and pay your firm. Such was not the case in the Wilke letter. You are an employee of your firm and apparently employ a secretary as well. A review of your recent filings (Form 620) shows a very large lump sum "general lobbying expense" category. On your Form 620 report this is not broken down to show payments to your secretary, for office rental, phones, general meals and support, etc. This information would be properly disclosed if you were filing also as a retained entity. Consequently, it is our conclusion that you must file reports as a retained entity as well as the lobbyist reports. However, you need not file both Form 620 and 650. The Commission has a form (Form 660 - Consolidated Report of Lobbyist and Lobbyist Employer) which
